instruction: How do I design cache-friendly data structures in C++? Explain the performance impact of data layout on modern CPUs.
output:
<think>
Cache-friendly data structures are one of the most impactful performance optimizations in C++. Modern CPUs have a memory hierarchy: L1 (~1ns), L2 (~3-5ns), L3 (~10-20ns), RAM (~60-100ns). A cache miss to RAM costs 50-100x more than an L1 hit. Cache lines are typically 64 bytes.

The key principles:
1. Spatial locality: access memory sequentially (arrays > linked lists)
2. Temporal locality: access the same data repeatedly in a short time
3. Data-oriented design: organize data by how it's accessed, not by object hierarchy
4. Structure of Arrays (SoA) vs Array of Structures (AoS): SoA is better when you process one field at a time across many objects; AoS is better when you access all fields of one object together

The classic example: iterating over a std::vector<int> vs std::list<int>. The vector is contiguous in memory so the CPU prefetcher loads the next cache line automatically. The list has nodes scattered across the heap, causing a cache miss per node.

For game engines and data processing, Entity Component System (ECS) architecture is the poster child of data-oriented design. Instead of:
  struct Entity { Position pos; Velocity vel; Health hp; Sprite sprite; }
You store:
  std::vector<Position> positions;
  std::vector<Velocity> velocities;
This way, updating all positions touches only position data (dense, cache-friendly).

I should also cover: hot/cold splitting (separate frequently-accessed fields from rarely-accessed ones), padding/alignment, and false sharing in multithreaded code.

The performance differences are dramatic: 10-50x for iteration-heavy workloads.
</think>

Here's how data layout affects performance and how to design cache-friendly structures:

```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <list>
#include <array>
#include <chrono>
#include <iostream>
#include <cstring>
#include <numeric>

// ============================================================
// 1. Sequential vs random access: vector vs list
// ============================================================

void benchmark_sequential_vs_random() {
    constexpr int N = 1'000'000;
    
    // Vector: contiguous memory, prefetcher-friendly
    std::vector<int> vec(N);
    std::iota(vec.begin(), vec.end(), 0);
    
    // List: scattered heap nodes, cache-miss per element
    std::list<int> lst(vec.begin(), vec.end());
    
    // Benchmark vector sum
    auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    long long sum_vec = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; ++i) {
        for (auto v : vec) sum_vec += v;
    }
    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    // Benchmark list sum
    long long sum_lst = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; ++i) {
        for (auto v : lst) sum_lst += v;
    }
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    auto vec_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t1 - t0).count();
    auto lst_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t2 - t1).count();
    
    std::cout << "Vector sum: " << vec_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "List sum:   " << lst_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "Ratio:      " << lst_ms / vec_ms << "x slower\n";
    // Typical result: list is 10-50x slower
}

// ============================================================
// 2. AoS vs SoA (Array of Structs vs Struct of Arrays)
// ============================================================

// AoS: good when you access ALL fields of each entity
struct ParticleAoS {
    float x, y, z;       // Position
    float vx, vy, vz;    // Velocity
    float mass;
    float lifetime;
    uint32_t color;
    uint32_t flags;
    // 40 bytes per particle — but if you only need x,y,z
    // you load 40 bytes to get 12 bytes of useful data (30% utilization)
};

// SoA: good when you process ONE field across all entities
struct ParticlesSoA {
    std::vector<float> x, y, z;        // Positions contiguous
    std::vector<float> vx, vy, vz;     // Velocities contiguous
    std::vector<float> mass;
    std::vector<float> lifetime;
    std::vector<uint32_t> color;
    std::vector<uint32_t> flags;
    
    void resize(size_t n) {
        x.resize(n); y.resize(n); z.resize(n);
        vx.resize(n); vy.resize(n); vz.resize(n);
        mass.resize(n); lifetime.resize(n);
        color.resize(n); flags.resize(n);
    }
};

void update_positions_aos(std::vector<ParticleAoS>& particles, float dt) {
    // Each iteration loads 40-byte struct, uses only 24 bytes (x,y,z,vx,vy,vz)
    // Cache lines wasted on mass, lifetime, color, flags
    for (auto& p : particles) {
        p.x += p.vx * dt;
        p.y += p.vy * dt;
        p.z += p.vz * dt;
    }
}

void update_positions_soa(ParticlesSoA& p, float dt) {
    const size_t n = p.x.size();
    // Each loop touches ONLY position and velocity arrays
    // 100% cache line utilization, auto-vectorizable
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        p.x[i] += p.vx[i] * dt;
        p.y[i] += p.vy[i] * dt;
        p.z[i] += p.vz[i] * dt;
    }
}

void benchmark_aos_vs_soa() {
    constexpr int N = 1'000'000;
    constexpr float dt = 0.016f;
    
    // AoS
    std::vector<ParticleAoS> aos(N);
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        aos[i] = {float(i), float(i), float(i), 1.f, 1.f, 1.f, 1.f, 10.f, 0, 0};
    }
    
    auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    for (int r = 0; r < 100; ++r) update_positions_aos(aos, dt);
    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    // SoA
    ParticlesSoA soa;
    soa.resize(N);
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        soa.x[i] = soa.y[i] = soa.z[i] = float(i);
        soa.vx[i] = soa.vy[i] = soa.vz[i] = 1.f;
    }
    
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    for (int r = 0; r < 100; ++r) update_positions_soa(soa, dt);
    auto t3 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    auto aos_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t1 - t0).count();
    auto soa_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t3 - t2).count();
    
    std::cout << "\nAoS position update: " << aos_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "SoA position update: " << soa_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "Speedup: " << aos_ms / soa_ms << "x\n";
}

// ============================================================
// 3. Hot/Cold splitting
// ============================================================

// BAD: rarely-used fields pollute cache lines
struct EntityBad {
    float x, y;           // Hot: accessed every frame
    float vx, vy;         // Hot: accessed every frame
    char name[64];        // Cold: accessed only for UI
    char description[256]; // Cold: almost never accessed
    // 332 bytes — hot data mixed with cold data
};

// GOOD: separate hot and cold data
struct EntityHot {
    float x, y, vx, vy;   // 16 bytes — fits in one cache line
    uint32_t cold_index;   // Index into cold storage
};

struct EntityCold {
    char name[64];
    char description[256];
};

// Hot path only touches EntityHot array — maximum cache efficiency
void update_entities(std::vector<EntityHot>& entities, float dt) {
    for (auto& e : entities) {
        e.x += e.vx * dt;
        e.y += e.vy * dt;
    }
}

// ============================================================
// 4. False sharing in multithreaded code
// ============================================================

// BAD: counters on the same cache line → false sharing
struct CountersBad {
    std::atomic<int64_t> count_a;  // Thread 1 writes
    std::atomic<int64_t> count_b;  // Thread 2 writes
    // Both on the same 64-byte cache line!
    // Each write invalidates the other core's cache line
};

// GOOD: pad to separate cache lines
struct alignas(64) PaddedCounter {
    std::atomic<int64_t> value;
};

struct CountersGood {
    PaddedCounter count_a;  // Own cache line
    PaddedCounter count_b;  // Own cache line
};

int main() {
    benchmark_sequential_vs_random();
    benchmark_aos_vs_soa();
    return 0;
}
```

Key principles: prefer contiguous arrays over pointer-chasing structures, use SoA layout when processing one field across many objects, split hot and cold data to maximize cache line utilization, and pad shared-memory counters to avoid false sharing. The performance difference is typically 5-50x for iteration-heavy workloads.

instruction: How does branch prediction affect C++ performance, and how can I write branch-prediction-friendly code?
output:
<think>
Branch prediction is a CPU optimization where the processor guesses the outcome of a conditional branch before it's evaluated, so it can continue executing instructions speculatively. Modern CPUs have sophisticated predictors (TAGE, perceptron-based) that achieve >95% accuracy on most code.

When the prediction is correct: zero cost — the pipeline continues at full speed.
When wrong (misprediction): 10-20 cycle penalty — the pipeline is flushed, speculative work is discarded.

At 4 GHz, a 15-cycle misprediction costs ~4ns. If you're iterating over millions of elements with a poorly predicted branch, that's millions of mispredictions = catastrophic performance.

Patterns that predict well:
1. Loops (always predict "take the back-edge") — nearly 100% accuracy
2. Branches that are almost always true or almost always false
3. Regular patterns (alternating, periodic)

Patterns that predict poorly:
1. Random/data-dependent branches (if data[i] > threshold with random data)
2. Branches with ~50/50 distribution
3. Branches where the pattern changes frequently

Techniques to avoid misprediction:
1. Branchless code using conditional moves (cmov)
2. Sorting data to make branches predictable
3. Lookup tables instead of if/else chains
4. Arithmetic tricks (use multiplication by 0/1 instead of if/else)
5. SIMD mask operations

The classic benchmark: summing elements > 128 in a sorted vs unsorted array. The sorted version is 5-6x faster because the branch predictor can learn the pattern.

I should also mention __builtin_expect (likely/unlikely) which doesn't help prediction accuracy but helps code layout — the likely path is placed inline for better icache behavior.
</think>

Here's how branch prediction affects C++ performance and how to write prediction-friendly code:

```cpp
#include <algorithm>
#include <array>
#include <chrono>
#include <cstdlib>
#include <iostream>
#include <numeric>
#include <random>
#include <vector>

// ============================================================
// 1. The classic: sorted vs unsorted branch prediction
// ============================================================

long long sum_above_threshold_branchy(const std::vector<int>& data, int threshold) {
    long long sum = 0;
    for (int val : data) {
        if (val >= threshold) {  // Branch: predictable if sorted
            sum += val;
        }
    }
    return sum;
}

void benchmark_sorted_vs_unsorted() {
    constexpr int N = 10'000'000;
    std::vector<int> data(N);
    
    std::mt19937 rng(42);
    std::uniform_int_distribution<int> dist(0, 255);
    for (auto& v : data) v = dist(rng);
    
    // Unsorted: branches are random → ~50% misprediction
    auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    auto sum1 = sum_above_threshold_branchy(data, 128);
    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    // Sorted: branches are predictable (all < 128 first, then all >= 128)
    std::sort(data.begin(), data.end());
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    auto sum2 = sum_above_threshold_branchy(data, 128);
    auto t3 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    auto unsorted_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t1 - t0).count();
    auto sorted_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t3 - t2).count();
    
    std::cout << "Unsorted: " << unsorted_ms << "ms (sum=" << sum1 << ")\n";
    std::cout << "Sorted:   " << sorted_ms << "ms (sum=" << sum2 << ")\n";
    std::cout << "Speedup:  " << unsorted_ms / sorted_ms << "x\n";
    // Typical: sorted is 4-6x faster
}

// ============================================================
// 2. Branchless alternatives
// ============================================================

// Branchless conditional sum using arithmetic
long long sum_above_branchless(const std::vector<int>& data, int threshold) {
    long long sum = 0;
    for (int val : data) {
        // No branch: multiply by 0 or 1
        // (val >= threshold) evaluates to 0 or 1
        sum += val * (val >= threshold);
    }
    return sum;
}

// Branchless min/max
inline int branchless_min(int a, int b) {
    // Compiler often generates cmov for std::min, but this is explicit
    return b + ((a - b) & ((a - b) >> 31));
}

inline int branchless_max(int a, int b) {
    return a - ((a - b) & ((a - b) >> 31));
}

// Branchless clamp
inline int branchless_clamp(int val, int lo, int hi) {
    // Modern compilers optimize std::clamp to cmov, but this guarantees it
    val = val < lo ? lo : val;  // Compiler → cmov
    val = val > hi ? hi : val;  // Compiler → cmov
    return val;
}

// ============================================================
// 3. Lookup tables vs if/else chains
// ============================================================

// BAD: unpredictable branches for categorization
int categorize_branchy(int value) {
    if (value < 10) return 0;
    if (value < 50) return 1;
    if (value < 100) return 2;
    if (value < 200) return 3;
    return 4;
}

// GOOD: lookup table — zero branches
int categorize_table(int value) {
    // Pre-computed for values 0-255
    static const int table[256] = []{
        std::array<int, 256> t{};
        for (int i = 0; i < 256; ++i) {
            if (i < 10) t[i] = 0;
            else if (i < 50) t[i] = 1;
            else if (i < 100) t[i] = 2;
            else if (i < 200) t[i] = 3;
            else t[i] = 4;
        }
        int result[256];
        std::copy(t.begin(), t.end(), result);
        return result;
    }();
    
    return (value >= 0 && value < 256) ? table[value] : 4;
}

// ============================================================
// 4. likely/unlikely hints for code layout
// ============================================================

#define LIKELY(x)   __builtin_expect(!!(x), 1)
#define UNLIKELY(x) __builtin_expect(!!(x), 0)
// C++20: [[likely]] and [[unlikely]] attributes

void process_with_hints(const std::vector<int>& data) {
    for (int val : data) {
        if (UNLIKELY(val < 0)) {
            // Error handling — rarely taken
            // Compiler places this code out-of-line (better icache)
            handle_error(val);
        } else [[likely]] {
            // Normal path — placed inline for better instruction cache
            process_normal(val);
        }
    }
}

void handle_error(int val) {
    std::cerr << "Error: " << val << "\n";
}

void process_normal(int val) {
    // Normal processing
}

// ============================================================
// 5. Benchmark: branchy vs branchless
// ============================================================

void benchmark_branchless() {
    constexpr int N = 10'000'000;
    std::vector<int> data(N);
    std::mt19937 rng(42);
    std::uniform_int_distribution<int> dist(0, 255);
    for (auto& v : data) v = dist(rng);
    
    // Branchy (random data → mispredictions)
    auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    volatile long long s1 = 0;
    for (int r = 0; r < 10; ++r)
        s1 = sum_above_threshold_branchy(data, 128);
    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    // Branchless
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    volatile long long s2 = 0;
    for (int r = 0; r < 10; ++r)
        s2 = sum_above_branchless(data, 128);
    auto t3 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    auto branchy_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t1 - t0).count();
    auto branchless_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t3 - t2).count();
    
    std::cout << "\nBranchy (random):   " << branchy_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "Branchless:         " << branchless_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "Speedup:            " << branchy_ms / branchless_ms << "x\n";
}

int main() {
    benchmark_sorted_vs_unsorted();
    benchmark_branchless();
    return 0;
}
```

Key takeaways: sort data before branching on it when possible, use branchless arithmetic (`val * (condition)`) for data-dependent operations, use lookup tables for categorization, and mark error paths with `[[unlikely]]`. Misprediction costs ~15 cycles per occurrence — with millions of elements, this dominates runtime.

instruction: How does RAII interact with performance in C++? Can RAII patterns have zero overhead compared to manual resource management?
output:
<think>
RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) is C++'s pattern for deterministic resource management. The question is whether wrapping resources in RAII classes has any performance cost compared to manual malloc/free, fopen/fclose, etc.

The answer is: zero overhead in most cases. The compiler inlines destructors, elides copies, and optimizes away wrapper objects entirely. RAII is a compile-time abstraction — at the machine code level, it generates the same instructions as manual management.

Where RAII can add cost:
1. Exception handling: RAII objects on the stack are destroyed during stack unwinding. If exceptions are enabled, the compiler must generate unwind tables. The tables themselves don't cost anything at runtime (table-based EH), but they do increase binary size.
2. Non-inlined destructors: if the destructor is in a separate translation unit, the compiler can't inline it → function call overhead.
3. Unnecessary copies: if RAII objects are copied when they should be moved (before C++11 or with deleted move constructors).
4. Virtual destructors: `std::unique_ptr<Base>` with virtual dtor has vtable overhead.

Where RAII is BETTER than manual:
1. Exception safety: manual code either leaks on exceptions or requires complex try/catch. RAII handles it automatically.
2. Scope-based cleanup prevents leaks from early returns, breaks, continues.
3. Move semantics: unique_ptr<T> can be moved zero-cost. Manual code requires explicit ownership transfer.

I should show: (1) lock_guard vs manual mutex, (2) unique_ptr vs raw pointer, (3) scope guard pattern, (4) assembly comparison showing zero overhead.

Also important: RAII enables the compiler to reason about object lifetimes, which can enable optimizations that aren't possible with manual code (like eliding the destructor when it provably does nothing, or reordering operations).
</think>

RAII is zero-overhead in practice — here's why and how to verify:

```cpp
#include <chrono>
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <mutex>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdlib>

// ============================================================
// 1. unique_ptr vs raw pointer — zero overhead
// ============================================================

struct Widget {
    int data[4];
    int compute() const { return data[0] + data[1] + data[2] + data[3]; }
};

// Manual management
long long sum_raw(int n) {
    long long sum = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        Widget* w = new Widget{{i, i+1, i+2, i+3}};
        sum += w->compute();
        delete w;
    }
    return sum;
}

// RAII with unique_ptr
long long sum_unique(int n) {
    long long sum = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        auto w = std::make_unique<Widget>(Widget{{i, i+1, i+2, i+3}});
        sum += w->compute();
        // Destructor calls delete automatically
    }
    return sum;
}

// Both compile to identical assembly with -O2:
//   new → compute → delete, in a loop.
// unique_ptr's destructor is inlined to a single `delete` call.
// Verify: g++ -O2 -S -o - raii.cpp | c++filt

// ============================================================
// 2. lock_guard vs manual lock/unlock — zero overhead
// ============================================================

std::mutex mtx;
int shared_counter = 0;

void increment_manual(int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        mtx.lock();
        ++shared_counter;
        mtx.unlock();
        // BUG RISK: if code between lock/unlock throws, mutex stays locked!
    }
}

void increment_raii(int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mtx);
        ++shared_counter;
        // lock_guard destructor calls unlock
        // Exception-safe: unlock happens even if ++ throws (it won't, but pattern matters)
    }
}

// Assembly is identical: lock_guard constructor = mutex.lock(),
// destructor = mutex.unlock(). Both inlined.

// ============================================================
// 3. Scope guard — RAII for arbitrary cleanup
// ============================================================

template<typename F>
class ScopeGuard {
    F func_;
    bool active_;
public:
    explicit ScopeGuard(F f) : func_(std::move(f)), active_(true) {}
    ~ScopeGuard() { if (active_) func_(); }
    
    void dismiss() { active_ = false; }
    
    ScopeGuard(ScopeGuard&& other) noexcept
        : func_(std::move(other.func_)), active_(other.active_) {
        other.active_ = false;
    }
    
    ScopeGuard(const ScopeGuard&) = delete;
    ScopeGuard& operator=(const ScopeGuard&) = delete;
};

template<typename F>
ScopeGuard<F> make_scope_guard(F f) {
    return ScopeGuard<F>(std::move(f));
}

void database_transaction() {
    // begin_transaction();
    auto rollback = make_scope_guard([]{
        // rollback_transaction();  // Called if we don't dismiss
        std::cout << "Rolling back\n";
    });
    
    // Do work that might fail...
    bool success = true; // simulate
    
    if (success) {
        // commit_transaction();
        rollback.dismiss();  // Don't rollback — we committed
    }
    // If success is false, destructor calls rollback automatically
}

// ============================================================
// 4. RAII advantages manual code CANNOT match
// ============================================================

// Manual code with multiple resources — error prone
void manual_multi_resource() {
    FILE* f1 = fopen("a.txt", "r");
    if (!f1) return;
    
    FILE* f2 = fopen("b.txt", "r");
    if (!f2) {
        fclose(f1);  // Must remember to clean up f1!
        return;
    }
    
    void* buf = malloc(4096);
    if (!buf) {
        fclose(f2);  // Must clean up both f1 and f2!
        fclose(f1);
        return;
    }
    
    // Use resources...
    
    free(buf);
    fclose(f2);
    fclose(f1);
    // If ANY line between allocation and cleanup throws: LEAK
}

// RAII: clean, exception-safe, zero overhead
struct FileHandle {
    FILE* fp;
    explicit FileHandle(const char* path, const char* mode)
        : fp(fopen(path, mode)) {}
    ~FileHandle() { if (fp) fclose(fp); }
    operator FILE*() const { return fp; }
    explicit operator bool() const { return fp != nullptr; }
    
    FileHandle(const FileHandle&) = delete;
    FileHandle& operator=(const FileHandle&) = delete;
    FileHandle(FileHandle&& other) noexcept : fp(other.fp) { other.fp = nullptr; }
};

void raii_multi_resource() {
    FileHandle f1("a.txt", "r");
    if (!f1) return;  // No cleanup needed
    
    FileHandle f2("b.txt", "r");
    if (!f2) return;  // f1 automatically closed
    
    auto buf = std::make_unique<char[]>(4096);
    
    // Use resources...
    // ALL cleanup is automatic, in correct reverse order
    // Exception safe: any throw cleans up everything
}

// ============================================================
// 5. When RAII has (small) overhead
// ============================================================

// Case 1: shared_ptr — reference counting IS overhead
void shared_ptr_cost() {
    // shared_ptr has:
    // - Atomic reference count increment/decrement (~5ns each)
    // - Extra allocation for control block (or use make_shared)
    // - 2x pointer size (ptr + control block ptr)
    auto sp = std::make_shared<Widget>(Widget{{1,2,3,4}});
    // For single-owner: use unique_ptr (zero overhead)
    // For shared ownership: shared_ptr cost is the price of correctness
}

// Case 2: Virtual destructor in base class
struct Base {
    virtual ~Base() = default;  // vtable pointer = 8 bytes per object
    virtual int value() const = 0;
};

struct Derived : Base {
    int val;
    int value() const override { return val; }
};

// unique_ptr<Base> uses virtual destructor — tiny overhead
// But this is the cost of polymorphism, not RAII

// ============================================================
// 6. Benchmark proving zero overhead
// ============================================================

void benchmark_raii_overhead() {
    constexpr int N = 10'000'000;
    
    auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    volatile long long s1 = sum_raw(N);
    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    volatile long long s2 = sum_unique(N);
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    auto raw_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t1 - t0).count();
    auto unique_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t2 - t1).count();
    
    std::cout << "Raw pointer:    " << raw_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "unique_ptr:     " << unique_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "Overhead: " << ((unique_ms / raw_ms) - 1.0) * 100 << "%\n";
    // Expected: ~0% overhead (within measurement noise)
}

int main() {
    benchmark_raii_overhead();
    database_transaction();
    return 0;
}
```

RAII is a zero-cost abstraction: `unique_ptr`, `lock_guard`, and custom RAII wrappers compile to identical machine code as manual management. The only RAII pattern with runtime cost is `shared_ptr` (atomic reference counting). The benefit is exception safety and leak prevention that manual code cannot reliably provide.

instruction: How do move semantics improve C++ performance, and what are the common pitfalls? Show the difference between copy and move operations.
output:
<think>
Move semantics (C++11) solve the problem of expensive unnecessary copies. The key insight: when an object is about to be destroyed (it's an rvalue), instead of copying its resources and then destroying the original, we can just steal (move) the resources. This turns O(n) copies into O(1) moves.

The most common case: returning large objects from functions. Pre-C++11, returning a std::vector<int> from a function would copy all elements. With move semantics, the vector's internal pointer is simply transferred to the caller.

How moves work mechanically:
- Copy: allocate new buffer, memcpy all elements, both old and new object are valid
- Move: steal the pointer from the source, set source's pointer to null, source is in a "valid but unspecified" state

Key concepts:
- Rvalue references (T&&): bind to temporaries/expiring objects
- std::move(): doesn't move anything — it's just a cast to T&& (tells the compiler "I'm done with this, you can steal from it")
- Move constructor: T(T&& other) noexcept
- Move assignment: T& operator=(T&& other) noexcept

Pitfalls:
1. Using an object after std::move — it's in an unspecified state
2. Forgetting noexcept on move operations — std::vector won't use your move constructor for reallocation if it's not noexcept
3. Returning by std::move — prevents NRVO (Named Return Value Optimization)
4. Moving from const objects — const T&& binds but can't actually move (falls back to copy)
5. Not implementing move operations when your class manages resources

I should show: (1) the performance difference between copy and move for a buffer class, (2) the noexcept issue, (3) the NRVO anti-pattern, (4) perfect forwarding.
</think>

Here's how move semantics work and the common mistakes to avoid:

```cpp
#include <algorithm>
#include <chrono>
#include <cstring>
#include <iostream>
#include <utility>
#include <vector>

// ============================================================
// 1. Custom class showing copy vs move
// ============================================================

class Buffer {
    char* data_;
    size_t size_;
    
public:
    explicit Buffer(size_t size)
        : data_(new char[size]), size_(size) {
        std::memset(data_, 0, size);
    }
    
    // Copy constructor: O(n) — allocates and copies everything
    Buffer(const Buffer& other)
        : data_(new char[other.size_]), size_(other.size_) {
        std::memcpy(data_, other.data_, size_);
        // std::cout << "COPY " << size_ << " bytes\n";
    }
    
    // Move constructor: O(1) — steals the pointer
    Buffer(Buffer&& other) noexcept
        : data_(other.data_), size_(other.size_) {
        other.data_ = nullptr;  // Source gives up ownership
        other.size_ = 0;
        // std::cout << "MOVE " << size_ << " bytes\n";
    }
    
    // Copy assignment
    Buffer& operator=(const Buffer& other) {
        if (this != &other) {
            delete[] data_;
            data_ = new char[other.size_];
            size_ = other.size_;
            std::memcpy(data_, other.data_, size_);
        }
        return *this;
    }
    
    // Move assignment: O(1)
    Buffer& operator=(Buffer&& other) noexcept {
        if (this != &other) {
            delete[] data_;
            data_ = other.data_;
            size_ = other.size_;
            other.data_ = nullptr;
            other.size_ = 0;
        }
        return *this;
    }
    
    ~Buffer() { delete[] data_; }
    
    size_t size() const { return size_; }
};

// ============================================================
// 2. Performance difference
// ============================================================

void benchmark_copy_vs_move() {
    constexpr size_t BUF_SIZE = 10'000'000; // 10MB
    constexpr int ITERATIONS = 100;
    
    Buffer original(BUF_SIZE);
    
    // Copy benchmark
    auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    for (int i = 0; i < ITERATIONS; ++i) {
        Buffer copy(original);  // Copy: allocate 10MB + memcpy 10MB
        (void)copy;
    }
    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    // Move benchmark
    std::vector<Buffer> sources;
    for (int i = 0; i < ITERATIONS; ++i) {
        sources.emplace_back(BUF_SIZE);
    }
    
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    for (int i = 0; i < ITERATIONS; ++i) {
        Buffer moved(std::move(sources[i]));  // Move: swap 2 pointers
        (void)moved;
    }
    auto t3 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    
    auto copy_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t1 - t0).count();
    auto move_ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t3 - t2).count();
    
    std::cout << "Copy " << ITERATIONS << "x 10MB: " << copy_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "Move " << ITERATIONS << "x 10MB: " << move_ms << "ms\n";
    std::cout << "Speedup: " << copy_ms / move_ms << "x\n";
    // Move is typically 1000-10000x faster for large buffers
}

// ============================================================
// 3. PITFALL: noexcept matters for std::vector
// ============================================================

class BadMover {
    int* data_;
public:
    BadMover() : data_(new int(42)) {}
    BadMover(const BadMover& o) : data_(new int(*o.data_)) {}
    
    // Missing noexcept! std::vector will COPY instead of MOVE
    // during reallocation to maintain strong exception guarantee.
    BadMover(BadMover&& o) /* noexcept missing */ : data_(o.data_) {
        o.data_ = nullptr;
    }
    
    ~BadMover() { delete data_; }
};

class GoodMover {
    int* data_;
public:
    GoodMover() : data_(new int(42)) {}
    GoodMover(const GoodMover& o) : data_(new int(*o.data_)) {}
    
    // noexcept → std::vector uses move during reallocation
    GoodMover(GoodMover&& o) noexcept : data_(o.data_) {
        o.data_ = nullptr;
    }
    
    ~GoodMover() { delete data_; }
};

// ============================================================
// 4. PITFALL: Don't return with std::move — kills NRVO
// ============================================================

std::vector<int> make_vector_good() {
    std::vector<int> v = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
    return v;  // NRVO: constructed directly in caller's stack frame
               // Zero copies, zero moves
}

std::vector<int> make_vector_bad() {
    std::vector<int> v = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
    return std::move(v);  // PREVENTS NRVO! Forces a move instead.
    // std::move here makes it WORSE, not better.
}

// ============================================================
// 5. PITFALL: Using object after std::move
// ============================================================

void use_after_move_bug() {
    std::vector<int> data = {1, 2, 3};
    std::vector<int> other = std::move(data);
    
    // BUG: data is in a "valid but unspecified" state
    // data.size() could be 0 or anything
    // data[0] is undefined behavior
    // std::cout << data[0];  // DON'T DO THIS
    
    // OK: you CAN reassign or clear a moved-from object
    data = {4, 5, 6};  // This is fine
    data.clear();       // This is fine too
}

// ============================================================
// 6. Perfect forwarding with std::forward
// ============================================================

class ResourceManager {
    std::vector<Buffer> resources_;
    
public:
    // Without forwarding: always copies
    void add_copy(const Buffer& buf) {
        resources_.push_back(buf);  // Always copies
    }
    
    // With perfect forwarding: copies lvalues, moves rvalues
    template<typename T>
    void add(T&& buf) {
        resources_.push_back(std::forward<T>(buf));
        // If buf is lvalue: forward<T> returns T& → copy
        // If buf is rvalue: forward<T> returns T&& → move
    }
};

void forwarding_demo() {
    ResourceManager rm;
    Buffer b(1024);
    
    rm.add(b);              // T=Buffer&, forward returns Buffer& → COPY
    rm.add(std::move(b));   // T=Buffer,  forward returns Buffer&& → MOVE
    rm.add(Buffer(2048));   // T=Buffer,  forward returns Buffer&& → MOVE
}

int main() {
    benchmark_copy_vs_move();
    forwarding_demo();
    return 0;
}
```

Key rules: (1) always mark move constructors/assignment `noexcept` — otherwise `std::vector` won't use them, (2) never `return std::move(local)` — it prevents NRVO, (3) never use an object after `std::move` except to reassign or destroy it, (4) use `std::forward` in templates to preserve value category. Move semantics turn O(n) copies into O(1) pointer swaps for any resource-owning class.
